_comment: Seed EN→KR cultural map. Stage 1 self-grows this with web+llm retrievals when persist=true.

4th of july.notes: 대한민국이 일본의 식민지_rule을 해방한 날을 기념하는 광복절

airport.notes: The everyday Korean word for airport, used naturally in conversation.

atm.notes: Koreans commonly refer to ATMs as 에이티엠.

bakery.notes: A family‑run rice‑cake shop, often a retirement business

bank.notes: Koreans commonly use the digital bank KakaoBank for everyday banking needs.

basketball.notes: Korean speakers would refer to soccer as the comparable school sport

big ben.notes: Iconic Seoul tower often cited as a must‑see landmark

bipolar disorder.notes: Commonly used Korean term for bipolar disorder in everyday discussion

birthday.notes: 생일은 일반적으로 축하하는 생일 행사를 의미합니다.

british museum.notes: Korea's main national museum, analogous to the British Museum

buckingham palace.notes: Korean royal palace that serves as a major tourist landmark

building.notes: 일상 대화에서 가장 자연스럽게 쓰는 단어

burgers.notes: 버거는 빵 사이에 고기 패티가 들어간 음식입니다.

burrito.notes: 김밥은 밥과 반찬을 말아 만든 한국식 랩입니다.

bus.notes: Koreans simply call city buses 버스.

caramel macchiato.notes: 에스프레소 위에 스팀밀크와 카라멜 소스를 얹은 음료

christianity.notes: Standard Korean term for Christianity used in religious discussions.

christmas.notes: romantic/couples event in Korea, not family-centric

clown.notes: Korean comedic performer analogous to a clown

coach.notes: Korean term for a sports team coach

college.notes: 수능은 대학 진학을 결정하는 국가 시험으로, 대학과 같은 진로 결정 단계와 유사하다.

converse.notes: Korea's cheaper Converse alternative, widely worn as casual sneakers.

customer service.notes: 일상 대화에서 고객을 도우미 역할을 하는 부서를 의미한다

dance.notes: Koreans commonly use the loanword '댄스' to refer to a dance performance or routine.

dance routine.notes: Korean term for a choreographed dance performance, commonly used in K‑pop and variety shows.

dishwashing.notes: Common term for washing dishes in Korean households

doctor.notes: Koreans refer to a medical doctor as 의사.

fam.notes: Used like 'bro' for close male friends, similar to 'fam' in English.

friendly competition.notes: Friendly matches are called 친선 경기 in Korean.

game.notes: Korea's championship basketball final, a major spectator event akin to a game.

god.notes: 신은 일반적인 신성 존재를 일컫는 말입니다.

halloween.notes: young-adult costume parties in urban areas

high school.notes: Korean term for high school, covering grades 10‑12

iphone.notes: Samsung의 플래그십 스마트폰 브랜드로, 아이폰과 같은 위상을 갖는다.

jeans.notes: Korean term for jeans, the usual word used when talking about pants.

jesus.notes: Korean Christians refer to Jesus as 예수님, the standard term used in religious contexts.

jesus christ.notes: Koreans typically call him 예수 그리스도 in religious contexts.

kindergarten.notes: Korean term for the early‑childhood education institution

london.notes: 서울 is the capital city Koreans commonly reference as a travel destination.

london heathrow.notes: Koreans commonly refer to Incheon International Airport as the primary international gateway.

lottery.notes: the most familiar national lottery game

man.notes: Used by females to address an older male friend informally.

marketing.notes: Commonly used in Korean business to denote product promotion and sales strategy.

measles.notes: Common Korean term for measles used in everyday discussion.

motorcycle.notes: Cars are the typical Korean equivalent when discussing giving away a personal vehicle and its upkeep costs.

national gallery.notes: Korean term for a national art museum/gallery

netflix.notes: 한국에서 스트리밍 서비스를 통틀어 통용되는 이름

outdoors.notes: Koreans use '자연' to denote the natural environment enjoyed in hiking, camping, and feeling connected to nature.

pet sitting.notes: Commonly used term for pet sitting services in Korea

phonies.notes: Korean slang for a fake or poser, used for insincere people.

porcelain.notes: Korean beauty term for flawless, glass‑like skin

prayer.notes: Common term for speaking to God in religious contexts

product.notes: Common term for a marketable item in Korean business

psychiatric facility.notes: Commonly used term for a psychiatric facility in Korea.

pumpkin spice.notes: 가을 디저트인 호박죽이 펌프킨 스파이스의 한국식 대체품입니다.

radio.notes: 대중이 라디오 방송을 듣는 일반적인 용어

rain.notes: Korean speakers refer to the rainy season (장마) when discussing continuous rain.

realtor.notes: Korea's most popular real‑estate listing app

roomie.notes: 친구와 같이 사는 사람을 비하하거나 친근하게 부르는 말

sandwich.notes: A common on‑the‑go snack often discarded half‑eaten.

stranger things.notes: ‘Strange Story’, the Korean title of the series.

therapy.notes: Korean mental‑health counseling app used for therapy

tour dates.notes: 공연 일정은 공연이나 투어의 날짜와 시간을 의미합니다.

tower of london.notes: Historic stone observatory tower, Korea’s iconic ancient tower.

toyota camry.notes: Korea's popular midsize sedan analogous to the Camry

train station.notes: Colloquially used to refer to a train station in everyday conversation.

turkey.notes: 오리는 한국의 명절 요리로 터키와 비슷한 역할을 해요.

vacation.notes: Korean term for a planned work‑or‑school break, used similarly to “vacation”.

war.notes: Korean term for war, used similarly to discuss global impacts.

westminster abbey.notes: Korean palace that is a major historic landmark, often cited alongside other iconic sites.

christ's.notes: Korean speakers refer to Jesus as 예수님 and his teachings as 예수님의 가르침.

hard time.notes: Commonly used to express personal difficulty and the need to talk it through.

forgiveness.notes: Koreans often refer to reconciliation (화해) when talking about letting go of resentment.

math test.notes: Korean high‑stakes college entrance exam that includes a math section

redemption.notes: Korean term for salvation, the cultural analogue of redemption.

relationship.notes: The term for a romantic partnership that Koreans commonly use in this context.

friends.notes: Koreans naturally use 친구 to refer to friends in personal contexts.

saturday.notes: Korean speakers refer to Saturday as 토요일

overnight.notes: Korean term for staying overnight, used when paying for lodging.

same old same old.notes: Casual reply meaning “nothing much, just the usual.”

profits.notes: Korean business contexts naturally use 이윤/이익 to denote profit.

tinsley.notes: A common Korean placeholder name used for a business partner.

guidance.notes: Koreans would ask for 상담 (counseling) in this context.
